local_names.japanese: ライム・ノジート（ノンアルコール・ライム・モクテル）

cuisine: Japanese (restaurant context); beverage of international origin

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for lime nojito or lime mocktail. Mapped to the closest parent non-alcoholic beverage class FOODON_03301638 as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Non-alcoholic beverages (FoodEx2 group AN — Soft drinks and other non-alcoholic beverages)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for lime nojito specifically. Mapped to the non-alcoholic beverages group AN000A as the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lime mocktail or nojito beverage type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass serving of approximately 300 g, as listed on the GrabFood menu for this item.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lime juice, raw (SR Legacy 09160) and Limeade, frozen concentrate, diluted with water (SR Legacy 14148); adapted composite for lime mocktail with sugar syrup
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for a lime nojito mocktail. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy lime juice (FDC ID 09160) and limeade (FDC ID 14148) composites, cross-referenced with the input GrabFood-derived per-100g values (energy 40 kcal, carbohydrate 10 g, sugar 9.33 g, protein 0.17 g, fat 0.0 g, sodium 3.33 mg, dietary fibre 0.17 g, calcium 3.33 mg, iron 0.03 mg, vitamin C 6.67 mg). The input values are consistent with a diluted lime juice and sugar syrup beverage and are retained as the best available estimate. fat_g is 0.0 g — confirmed naturally zero for this beverage type per USDA SR Legacy lime juice data. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 mg — naturally zero for a plant-based beverage. saturated_fat_g is 0.0 g — naturally zero. trans_fat_g is 0.0 g — naturally zero.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Sweetened lemonade and sugar-sweetened citrus beverage analogues.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a lime nojito mocktail. GI estimated at 58 (medium) by analogy with sweetened lemonade and sugar-sweetened citrus beverages reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The beverage contains 10 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly as sucrose/fructose from sugar syrup and lime juice. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (58 × 30 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 17.4 g GL (medium-high).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (9.33 g sugar/100 g); AHA guidelines identify SSBs as independently raising blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite very low sodium content (3.33 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this sugar-sweetened beverage; request an unsweetened version with lime juice, mint, and soda water only, as the AHA 2018 guidelines identify SSBs as independently raising BP risk regardless of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (9.33 g/100 g, predominantly added sucrose from sugar syrup) with negligible dietary fibre (0.17 g/100 g), resulting in rapid glucose absorption and a medium-to-high glycemic load of 17.4 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard form; the 30 g available carbohydrate per 300 g serving with a GI of approximately 58 delivers a glycemic load of 17.4, and liquid sugar absorbs faster than solid food — request no sugar syrup or substitute with a zero-calorie sweetener.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar load (9.33 g/100 g, approximately 28 g per serving) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and hepatic de novo lipogenesis at habitual intake levels; fat and saturated fat are zero, which is favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this beverage due to its high added sugar content (~28 g per 300 g serving), which at habitual intake is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines; the absence of fat and sodium is favourable but does not offset the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.33 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; vitamin C (6.67 mg/100 g) provides minor antioxidant benefit but is insufficient to offset the inflammatory sugar load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request the unsweetened version of this beverage, as the added sugar syrup promotes inflammatory cytokine activity; unsweetened lime and mint water would retain the vitamin C benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Lime Nojito (also called a Nojito or Virgin Lime Mojito) is a non-alcoholic mocktail inspired by the classic Cuban Mojito cocktail, served at a Japanese restaurant in Singapore. It is prepared by combining fresh lime juice, sugar syrup, mint leaves, and soda water or still water, mixed and served chilled over ice in a tall glass. The beverage has a bright, tart citrus flavour balanced by sweetness from the sugar syrup and a refreshing herbal note from the mint. Per 100 g it provides approximately 40 kcal, 10 g carbohydrate (9.33 g as sugar), 0.17 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 0.17 g dietary fibre, 3.33 mg sodium, 3.33 mg calcium, 0.03 mg iron, and 6.67 mg vitamin C. A standard 300 g glass serving delivers approximately 120 kcal and 30 g carbohydrate. Clinically, this is a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with a medium glycemic index (~58) and a glycemic load of approximately 17.4 per serving, making it unsuitable for patients with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened form. The beverage is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It contains no alcohol, no fat, and no cholesterol. The vitamin C content from lime juice provides minor antioxidant benefit. An unsweetened version (lime juice, mint, soda water, no sugar syrup) would be suitable for most dietary restrictions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lime mocktail?
Is lime nojito suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of a lime nojito?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for a lime nojito mocktail. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy lime juice (09160) and limeade (14148) composites and are consistent with the GrabFood-derived input values. GI is estimated by analogy with sweetened citrus beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to the adapted source match and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis for this specific beverage.
notes: fat_g is naturally zero — lime juice, sugar syrup, mint, and soda water contain no fat by composition; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy lime juice (FDC ID 09160) data. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all naturally zero for this plant-based beverage with no fat-containing ingredients. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero — plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. potassium_mg set to -1: detailed potassium data for this specific mixed beverage formulation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; estimated potassium from lime juice is approximately 30 mg/100 g but is not confirmed for this diluted preparation. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this mixed beverage are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. The beverage name '960D' appears to be a menu item code at the originating Japanese restaurant on GrabFood Singapore. The dish is classified as Japanese cuisine context only because it is served at a Japanese restaurant; the mocktail itself is of international origin. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened lemonade and citrus SSB data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Multilingual names added for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for Singapore-served dish context, in addition to the mandatory Japanese name for the Japanese restaurant cuisine context.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name 'Lime Nojito (Non-Alcoholic Lime Mocktail)'. Japanese local name provided per G6 mandatory rule for Japanese cuisine context. Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names added to satisfy multilingual compliance for Singapore-served context. Romanized form also provided.
